name: red pillows
shortDescription: Red pillows are soft trade goods with a bold color that turns plain rooms into places pretending they planned ahead. They are bought for guest beds, court benches, festival tents, and anyone who wants comfort to look a little expensive.
flavorText: “A red pillow makes even borrowed furniture look intentional.”

name: red and yellow bush
shortDescription: A red and yellow bush is a bright ornamental plant with enough color to pull the eye across a crowded market stall. Gardeners like its cheerful heat, while dyers and charmers argue over which shade is doing the real work.
flavorText: “Some bushes flower; this one announces.”

name: sea weed
shortDescription: Sea weed is slick, salty, and more useful than its smell first suggests. Coastal cooks dry it, healers steep it, sailors curse it in ropes, and clever merchants sell the same bundle under three different virtues.
flavorText: “The sea throws little away; it merely lets traders decide what to call it.”

name: scarce sharp lovey
shortDescription: Scarce sharp lovey is a strange, thorned plant with sweet color and unfriendly edges. It is popular in love charms, spite gifts, and gardens owned by people who believe affection should include a warning.
flavorText: “Sharp lovey is romance with the thorns left honest.”

name: small leaf
shortDescription: A small leaf seems humble until a healer asks for exactly that shape, that vein, and that fresh green edge. It dries quickly, bruises easily, and reminds apprentices that little ingredients can ruin large recipes.
flavorText: “Small leaves are where careful medicine learns humility.”

name: squiggle leafs
shortDescription: Squiggle leafs curl and twist as if the plant changed its mind while growing. Herbalists prize them for odd infusions, while market children prize them because they make serious botanists say silly things.
flavorText: “A squiggle leaf is proof that nature doodles in the margins.”

name: sugar cane
shortDescription: Sugar cane is tall, jointed sweetness hidden behind a fibrous green wall. It travels as stalks, syrup, gossip, and profit, because every kingdom finds a reason to tax anything that makes bitterness easier to swallow.
flavorText: “Sugar cane is sunlight taught to snap between the teeth.”

name: tall leaf plant
shortDescription: A tall leaf plant brings height, shade, and dramatic green shape to gardens that would otherwise crouch close to the soil. Its leaves are broad enough for shelter, wrapping, and the kind of decoration that makes courtyards feel cooler than they are.
flavorText: “Tall leaves do not hurry upward; they simply assume the sky has room.”

name: tri plant
shortDescription: The tri plant grows in neat threefold patterns, making it popular with charmers, shrine gardeners, and anyone who sees meaning in repeated leaves. Its practical use is modest, but symbols often sell better than medicine.
flavorText: “Three leaves are never just three leaves once a priest notices them.”

name: vine
shortDescription: Vine is flexible, climbing, and stubborn enough to turn walls, trees, and abandoned wagons into trellises without permission. Basket-makers, herbalists, vintners, and fence-menders all find uses for it, though none can make it stay where asked.
flavorText: “A vine is a road that refuses to lie flat.”

name: notable tally
shortDescription: Notable tally is a marked botanical stalk used by village counters, charm-workers, and old herbalists who track growth by cuts and knots. It looks simple, but its value lies in how neatly it turns memory into a physical thing.
flavorText: “A tally remembers what a merchant hopes no one checks.”

name: yellow blue bush
shortDescription: A yellow blue bush carries two bright colors at once, cheerful yellow against cooler blue like sunlight arguing with shade. Garden buyers love the contrast, and dyers enjoy pretending they can capture both without disappointment.
flavorText: “Yellow and blue on one bush make even bees look undecided.”

name: notable rayshine
shortDescription: Notable rayshine opens in narrow bright spokes, giving the impression of a tiny sun rehearsing for a festival. It is valued for dyes, garlands, and cheerful remedies sold to people who believe color can improve the body’s mood.
flavorText: “Rayshine is what a flower does when it wants applause from daylight.”

name: yellow zaphones
shortDescription: Yellow zaphones are odd golden blooms with a bell-like shape and a scent that clings lightly to fingers. They sell well in spring markets, especially to perfume sellers who claim the fragrance sounds brighter than it smells.
flavorText: “Zaphones ring only in the nose, which has never stopped poets.”

name: small rho
shortDescription: Small rho is a compact little herb known more by its curled form than by any grand reputation. It is the sort of ingredient healers buy quietly and novices overlook until the recipe fails without it.
flavorText: “Small rho proves that a humble curl can hold the whole remedy together.”

name: yellow pods
shortDescription: Yellow pods rattle softly when dry and stain the palm with a dusty gold when opened. Cooks, dyers, and seed merchants all inspect them differently, which lets a patient seller quote three different prices.
flavorText: “A pod is a purse the plant fills for itself.”

name: yellow red bush
shortDescription: A yellow red bush has the look of late summer caught between ripeness and flame. It is too lively for somber gardens, perfect for festival paths, and useful wherever a merchant wants color to do half the shouting.
flavorText: “Yellow red bushes make quiet courtyards look guilty of celebration.”

name: saguaro cactus
shortDescription: A saguaro cactus is a tall desert sentinel, slow-growing, water-wise, and shaped like it is forever deciding whether to wave. It is hard to transport well, but in dry country a living saguaro can mark shelter, patience, and stubborn wealth.
flavorText: “The saguaro raises its arms because the desert has been asking questions for centuries.”

name: golden barrel cactus
shortDescription: A golden barrel cactus is round, ribbed, and armored in bright spines that catch the sun like a warning made decorative. Gardeners prize it for its shape, collectors for its rarity, and thieves for teaching them caution.
flavorText: “A golden barrel cactus is treasure that learned self-defense.”

name: rose
shortDescription: A rose is the old reliable currency of romance, apology, mourning, and vanity. Its petals sell softness, its thorns preserve dignity, and its scent has talked buyers into worse decisions than any salesman.
flavorText: “The rose survives courtship by keeping a knife under every compliment.”

name: violet
shortDescription: A violet is a small, cool-colored flower with a shy face and a stronger presence than its size suggests. It is used in sweets, dyes, pressed keepsakes, and quiet gifts where a rose would arrive shouting.
flavorText: “Violets are what affection sends when it has learned manners.”

name: Crownfall seeds
shortDescription: Crownfall seeds are packed with the pride of hill farms, stone terraces, and growers who trust altitude to improve character. Buyers prize them for hardy crops and for the name, because a famous hillside sells even before it sprouts.
flavorText: “Crownfall farmers plant seed as if every furrow were a small inheritance.”

name: Goldreef seeds
shortDescription: Goldreef seeds come from bright coastal plots where salt wind, rich soil, and stubborn hands produce crops with a reputation for color. They are often bought by growers hoping a little famous luck can be buried and watered.
flavorText: “Goldreef seeds promise sunshine first and harvest second.”

name: Redvale seeds
shortDescription: Redvale seeds are practical, tough, and selected by farmers who have learned not to trust gentle seasons. They are less glamorous than courtly garden stock, but they sprout with the grim reliability of people who know winter by name.
flavorText: “Redvale does not breed pretty seed; it breeds seed that comes back.”

name: seedling broad tree
shortDescription: A broad tree seedling is a young promise of shade, timber, roots, and future arguments about property lines. Its leaves are already wide enough to suggest ambition, though it still fits in a careful basket.
flavorText: “Every broad tree begins as a thing a goat could ruin.”

name: seedling bush
shortDescription: A bush seedling is modest nursery stock, all green starts and hopeful twigs. It may become hedge, berry patch, windbreak, or nuisance, depending on the soil and the owner’s patience.
flavorText: “A bush seedling is a fence that has not learned politics yet.”

name: seedling curvy
shortDescription: A curvy seedling grows with a bend already in its stem, as if it intends to become decorative before becoming useful. Gardeners prize the unusual shape, while practical farmers mutter about whether crooked things bear straight fruit.
flavorText: “Some seedlings lean because the world looked more interesting sideways.”

name: seedling five leafs
shortDescription: A five-leaf seedling is small but eye-catching, its tidy spread giving charm-workers and gardeners plenty to interpret. It is often bought for luck, though luck at this age still needs water and protection from chickens.
flavorText: “Five leaves make a promise; the roots decide whether to keep it.”

name: seedling palm
shortDescription: A palm seedling is a narrow green start to a tree that dreams of heat, courtyards, and high leaves clattering in dry wind. It travels poorly in cold weather, but in the right climate it sells a whole image of shade.
flavorText: “A palm seedling is a desert postcard written in roots.”

name: seedling tree
shortDescription: A tree seedling is the most ordinary sort of future: thin stem, eager leaves, and a value that mostly belongs to years not yet lived. Farmers buy it for orchards, towns for avenues, and dreamers for reasons too large for the pot.
flavorText: “Planting a tree is trade with someone you may never meet.”

name: seedling two leafs
shortDescription: A two-leaf seedling is barely past its first declaration of life, simple enough to seem fragile and stubborn enough to prove otherwise. It is bought by growers who know every harvest starts as something unimpressive.
flavorText: “Two leaves are not much, unless they are the first two.”

name: islands republic seeds
shortDescription: Islands republic seeds are wrapped for humid gardens, salt air, and growers who know boats decide harvests as much as soil. They carry a reputation for bright produce, stubborn roots, and a little maritime independence in every sack.
flavorText: “Island seed learns early that every field has a shoreline somewhere.”

name: market seeds
shortDescription: Market seeds are mixed, practical planting stock sold by the scoop to buyers who need results more than pedigree. They are not noble, rare, or romantic, but they keep gardens busy and stew pots hopeful.
flavorText: “Market seeds are what people buy when hunger has no patience for lineage.”

name: wheat seed
shortDescription: Wheat seed is plain wealth waiting for rain, the beginning of bread, ale, tax ledgers, and village arguments about weather. It looks humble in the hand because it has not yet become everyone’s problem.
flavorText: “A kingdom can survive without jewels longer than it can survive without wheat.”

name: barley seed
shortDescription: Barley seed is hardy grain stock for fields, porridge, malt, and the dependable comfort of food that asks little from poor soil. Brewers respect it, farmers trust it, and horses approve without comment.
flavorText: “Barley is wheat’s rough cousin, and often the one who shows up.”

name: sorghum seed
shortDescription: Sorghum seed is tough, dry-country grain stock, valued where heat punishes delicate crops and water must be argued over. It is not glamorous, but it feeds people in places where glamour would wilt.
flavorText: “Sorghum is what a field plants when it has learned to distrust rain.”

name: large branch
shortDescription: A large branch is rough wood with shape already given by wind, weight, and weather. Carvers, firewood sellers, tool-makers, and children with heroic ambitions all see different futures in it.
flavorText: “A branch is timber before bureaucracy gets involved.”

name: log
shortDescription: A log is honest heavy stock: fuel, lumber, stool, bridge, barricade, or problem depending on who is carrying it. It has no mystery, which is why every market needs it anyway.
flavorText: “A log is a tree after negotiation with an axe.”

name: seedling bare
shortDescription: A bare seedling is a stripped little start of life, roots exposed and leaves absent or nearly so. It looks pitiful until planted well, which is a useful reminder that beginnings rarely dress for success.
flavorText: “Bare seedlings ask for faith before they offer shade.”

name: small branch
shortDescription: A small branch is light wood for kindling, handles, charms, bird cages, toy swords, and other minor works that keep hands busy. It is cheap because it is common, not because it lacks uses.
flavorText: “A small branch is only small until someone needs a peg.”

name: stump
shortDescription: A stump is the stubborn remainder of a tree, heavy with rings, roots, and the story of something cut short. It can become a chopping block, a stool, a carving base, or a quiet accusation in the middle of a field.
flavorText: “A stump is a tree that stopped rising but refused to leave.”

name: scarf
shortDescription: A scarf is a simple strip of warmth, color, and personal style, useful on cold roads and surprisingly eloquent in farewell scenes. Good cloth makes it practical; the way someone knots it makes it theirs.
flavorText: “A scarf remembers the weather and sometimes the person who gave it.”

name: ceremonial cloak
shortDescription: A ceremonial cloak is made to move slowly through important rooms and make witnesses believe something official is happening. Its value lies in fabric, trim, and the old truth that authority improves when it has a hemline.
flavorText: “Ceremony begins when ordinary cloth is told to behave historically.”

name: scarce dragonskin cloak
shortDescription: A scarce dragonskin cloak is supple, scaled, and expensive enough to make even arrogant buyers check their posture. Whether every scale is truly draconic is a question best asked after counting the guards near the seller.
flavorText: “Dragons rarely donate skin, which is why the cloak tells its story carefully.”

name: leather cloak
shortDescription: A leather cloak is roadwear for rain, thorn scrub, saddle work, and nights when wool would soak through before dawn. It is heavier than cloth but more forgiving of bad weather and worse company.
flavorText: “Leather cloaks are bought by people who expect the road to argue.”

name: ratskin cloak
shortDescription: A ratskin cloak is an unpleasant but clever garment pieced from small hides, usually worn by people with little money or very specific tastes. It sheds water better than expected and dignity worse than advertised.
flavorText: “A ratskin cloak keeps out the cold and invites every joke in the tavern.”

name: royal cloak
shortDescription: A royal cloak is rich cloth designed to make standing still look like a political act. It carries weight, color, lining, and expectation in equal measure, and is rarely worn by anyone allowed to be comfortable.
flavorText: “Royal cloaks do not warm the wearer; they warm the idea of the wearer.”

name: simple cloak
shortDescription: A simple cloak is the dependable friend of travelers, shepherds, watchmen, and anyone who owns more road than wardrobe. It is not fine, but it folds, wraps, hides, warms, and forgives rough handling.
flavorText: “A simple cloak is a roof you can carry.”

name: scarce cloak of starlight
shortDescription: A scarce cloak of starlight seems woven from dark cloth pricked with cold little gleams. It is beautiful in daylight and troubling after dusk, when the pattern looks less embroidered than borrowed from the sky.
flavorText: “Wear starlight carefully; the night may recognize its own.”

name: thiefs cloak
shortDescription: A thiefs cloak is cut for quiet movement, deep shadow, and pockets that do not introduce themselves. Honest buyers call it discreet travelwear; honest sellers do not believe them.
flavorText: “The best thiefs cloak is remembered only by the person missing something.”
